content.text: I want to check this repository for potential vulnerabilities, can you search for them, and apply fixes if you find any

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Search repository for vulnerabilities and apply fixes
Search for and fix repository vulnerabilities

content.thinking: The user wants me to do a security review of this OpenSSL 0.9.7 repository. Let me use the security-review skill.

content.content: Launching skill: security-review

content.text:
Tell the user: /security-review needs to run inside a git repository, but the current working directory (`c:\Users\Isidor\projects\CoSec\cosec-ai-main\unpatched\openssl-OpenSSL_0_9_7`) is not one.

If the repository is in a subdirectory, `cd` into it first and then re-run /security-review.

If this is a self-hosted runner session created without a `git_repository` source, either add one at session creation so the runner clones it and sets the working directory, or `cd` into the cloned repo before running the review.

content.thinking: The user wants to check for vulnerabilities in this OpenSSL 0.9.7 codebase. Since it's not a git repo, I'll do a manual security review by exploring the codebase for common vulnerabilities.

content.text: The `/security-review` skill requires a git repository. I'll perform a manual security review of this OpenSSL 0.9.7 codebase instead, searching for common vulnerability patterns and applying fixes where appropriate.

input.prompt:
Search for common security vulnerabilities in this OpenSSL 0.9.7 C codebase at c:\Users\Isidor\projects\CoSec\cosec-ai-main\unpatched\openssl-OpenSSL_0_9_7. Do a very thorough search for:

1. Buffer overflow patterns: strcpy, strcat, sprintf, gets, scanf without bounds checking
2. Integer overflow in memory allocation: malloc(n * size) without overflow check
3. Use of deprecated/unsafe functions: MD5, DES single, RC4 for key material
4. Format string vulnerabilities: printf(user_controlled)
5. Memory management: double-free patterns, use-after-free
6. SSL/TLS known issues: padding oracle patterns, version negotiation issues

For each finding, report:
- File path and line number
- The vulnerable code snippet
- Type of vulnerability
- Severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low)

Focus on the most impactful findings. Search broadly but report concisely. Limit to top 20 findings.

content.text:
Perfect. Now let me compile my comprehensive report based on all the vulnerabilities I found. Let me create a final summary:

Based on my thorough security vulnerability scan of the OpenSSL 0.9.7 codebase, here are the top 20 most impactful security findings:

## Critical Security Vulnerabilities in OpenSSL 0.9.7

### 1. **Buffer Overflow - strcpy without bounds checking**
- **File**: `crypto/conf/conf_def.c` (Line 238)
- **Code**: `strcpy(section,"default");`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: Unsafe strcpy on fixed-size stack buffer with no validation of size.

### 2. **Buffer Overflow - strcpy in DSO filename handling**
- **File**: `crypto/dso/dso_lib.c` (Line 386)
- **Code**: `strcpy(copied, filename);`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: Allocates `strlen(filename) + 1` but uses strcpy which can overflow if race condition exists.

### 3. **Buffer Overflow - strcpy in random file handling**
- **File**: `crypto/rand/randfile.c` (Line 180, 208, 223)
- **Code**: `strcpy(tmpf, file);` and `strcpy(buf,s);`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: Fixed-size buffer without validation of environment variable length.

### 4. **Buffer Overflow - strcpy in kssl.c (Kerberos)**
- **File**: `ssl/kssl.c` (Line 1637)
- **Code**: `strcpy(*string, text);`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: Allocates `strlen(text) + 1` via calloc but strcpy could overflow on untrusted input.

### 5. **Buffer Overflow - strcpy in CA application**
- **File**: `apps/ca.c` (Lines 577, 580, 1299, 1309, 1345, 1585)
- **Code**: Multiple `strcpy(buf[x], source);` operations
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: Multiple unsafe strcpy calls on fixed buffers handling file paths and serial numbers.

### 6. **Timing Attack - Non-constant time MAC verification**
- **File**: `ssl/s3_pkt.c` (Line 399)
- **Code**: `if (memcmp(md,&(rr->data[rr->length]),mac_size) != 0)`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Timing Attack / Information Disclosure
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: MAC verification uses standard memcmp which is not constant-time. Allows plaintext recovery via timing analysis.

### 7. **Timing Attack - Non-constant time finished message verification**
- **File**: `ssl/s3_both.c` (Line 223)
- **Code**: `if (memcmp(p, s->s3->tmp.peer_finish_md, i) != 0)`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Timing Attack
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: Finished message verification is not constant-time, enabling timing-based attacks.

### 8. **PKCS#1 Padding Oracle - Conditional Error Handling**
- **File**: `ssl/s3_clnt.c` (Lines 1476-1479) and `ssl/s2_clnt.c` (Lines 711-714)
- **Code**: PKCS1_CHECK code allows deliberate decryption errors
- **Vulnerability Type**: Padding Oracle / Bleichenbacher Attack
- **Severity**: Critical
- **Description**: Code allows intentional corruption of PKCS#1 padding via `SSL_OP_PKCS1_CHECK_*` flags, enabling padding oracle attacks. Comments explicitly reference Bleichenbacher's attack.

### 9. **Protocol Downgrade - SSLv2 enabled by default**
- **File**: `ssl/s23_clnt.c` (Lines 71-80)
- **Code**: SSLv23 method supports SSLv2 with no default protection
- **Vulnerability Type**: Protocol Downgrade Attack
- **Severity**: Critical
- **Description**: SSLv2 is enabled in version negotiation. SSLv2 is fundamentally broken with export ciphers and weak key material.

### 10. **DES encryption for key material**
- **File**: `crypto/des/destest.c`, `crypto/mdc2/mdc2dgst.c`, `apps/speed.c`
- **Code**: `DES_set_key_unchecked()` and `DES_ecb_encrypt()` used
- **Vulnerability Type**: Use of Weak Cryptography
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: DES (56-bit) is used for Kerberos keys and MDC2 digests. Single DES is cryptographically broken.

### 11. **RC4 support for key derivation**
- **File**: Multiple SSL/TLS files support RC4
- **Vulnerability Type**: Weak Cipher Support
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: RC4 is included as selectable cipher. RC4 stream cipher has known biases in key schedule and is broken.

### 12. **MD5 usage in certificate signatures**
- **File**: `ssl/kssl.c`, `ssl/s2_clnt.c` (Line 787: `SSL2_AT_MD5_WITH_RSA_ENCRYPTION`)
- **Vulnerability Type**: Weak Hash Function
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: MD5 is used for signatures (SSLv2). MD5 is cryptographically broken and prone to collision attacks.

### 13. **Unsafe memory operations - strcpy in s_socket.c**
- **File**: `crypto/rand/rand_egd.c` (Line 148)
- **Code**: `strcpy(addr.sun_path,path);`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: Fixed-size sun_path buffer without bounds checking on external input.

### 14. **Integer encoding issues - atoi without validation**
- **File**: `apps/ca.c` (Line 392, 460, 465)
- **Code**: `days=atoi(*(++argv));` without range checking
- **Vulnerability Type**: Integer Overflow / Validation Issue
- **Severity**: Medium
- **Description**: atoi on user input without validation of returned values or ranges.

### 15. **File permission race condition**
- **File**: `crypto/rand/randfile.c` (Lines 142-153)
- **Code**: `open(file, O_CREAT, 0600)` followed by `chmod(file,0600)`
- **Vulnerability Type**: TOCTTOU / Race Condition
- **Severity**: Medium
- **Description**: Comment explicitly states chmod is too late - file created with default permissions before chmod applied.

### 16. **Uninitialized memory in crypto operations**
- **File**: `crypto/rand/rand_egd.c`, various socket code
- **Vulnerability Type**: Information Disclosure
- **Severity**: Medium
- **Description**: Stack buffers used for cryptographic values without guaranteed initialization.

### 17. **strcat without bounds checking**
- **File**: `crypto/conf/conf_mod.c` (Lines 566, 568)
- **Code**: `strcat(file,"/");` and `strcat(file,OPENSSL_CONF);`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: Sequential strcat operations on fixed buffer without length validation.

### 18. **sprintf without size bounds**
- **File**: `crypto/asn1/a_time.c` (Lines 152-153)
- **Code**: `strcpy(str, "19");` / `strcpy(str, "20");` on allocated strings
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: Medium
- **Description**: Time string handling with potential for overflow on century conversion.

### 19. **BIO_gets on unbounded buffers**
- **File**: `crypto/bio/bss_file.c` (Line 305)
- **Code**: `fgets(buf,size,(FILE *)bp->ptr);`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Potential Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: Medium
- **Description**: BIO_gets wrapper around fgets may not properly validate size parameters in all code paths.

### 20. **Kerberos integration strcpy after malloc**
- **File**: `ssl/kssl.c` (Lines 1634-1637)
- **Code**: `strcpy(*string, text);` after `calloc(1, strlen(text) + 1)`
- **Vulnerability Type**: Buffer Overflow
- **Severity**: High
- **Description**: While malloc is sized correctly, untrusted input could be corrupted before strcpy, plus potential TOCTOU.

---

## Summary

This OpenSSL 0.9.7 codebase contains **multiple critical vulnerabilities** spanning buffer overflows, timing attacks, padding oracle issues, protocol weaknesses, and weak cryptography. The most severe issues are:

1. **Padding oracle attacks** via PKCS1_CHECK (Bleichenbacher) - Critical
2. **SSLv2 support** with broken cryptography - Critical  
3. **Timing attacks** on MAC and finish verification - High
4. **Widespread strcpy/strcat** buffer overflows - High
5. **DES/RC4/MD5** use in security-critical contexts - High

These vulnerabilities would allow attackers to conduct protocol downgrades, decrypt traffic, forge signatures, and potentially achieve remote code execution through buffer overflows.

This OpenSSL 0.9.7 codebase contains **multiple critical vulnerabilities** spanning buffer overflows, timing attacks, padding oracle issues, protocol weaknesses, and weak cryptography. The most severe issues are:

1. **Padding oracle attacks** via PKCS1_CHECK (Bleichenbacher) - Critical
2. **SSLv2 support** with broken cryptography - Critical

lastPrompt: I want to check this repository for potential vulnerabilities, can you search for them, and apply fixes if you find any

aiTitle: Search for and fix repository vulnerabilities

content.thinking:
Now let me look at the most critical and fixable vulnerabilities and apply patches. Let me focus on the most impactful ones that I can actually fix in code:

1. Timing attack in ssl/s3_pkt.c - replace memcmp with constant-time comparison
2. strcpy buffer overflows in key files
3. Padding oracle in ssl/s3_clnt.c

Let me read the relevant files first.

content.text: Good findings. Let me now read the most critical files and apply fixes, starting with the highest-severity issues.

content.thinking: Now I have a good picture of the key vulnerabilities. Let me also check the openssl/crypto directory for a constant-time memory comparison function that might already exist, since I'll need one to fix the timing attacks.

content.text: Now let me check if there's already a constant-time comparison utility in the codebase, then apply the fixes.

content.content: No files found

lastPrompt: I want to check this repository for potential vulnerabilities, can you search for them, and apply fixes if you find any

aiTitle: Search for and fix repository vulnerabilities

content.thinking:
Good. Now I have a clear picture. Let me apply the fixes:

1. **Critical: Timing attack in s3_pkt.c** - Replace memcmp with constant-time comparison for MAC check
2. **Critical: Timing attack in s3_both.c** - Replace memcmp with constant-time comparison for finished message
3. **Critical: PKCS1_CHECK padding oracle in s3_clnt.c** - Remove the PKCS1_CHECK code (it's ifdef'd but dangerous)
4. **High: randfile.c** - Fix the file permission race (O_CREAT should use O_CREAT|O_EXCL with correct permissions, and fix the strcpy with bounds checking)
5. **High: conf_mod.c** - The strcpy/strcat are actually safe since the buffer is malloc'd with the exact right size
6. **High: rand_egd.c** - The strcpy is already guarded by a strlen check

For the constant-time comparison, I'll add a utility function or inline it where needed. The most impactful fixes are the MAC timing attacks in s3_pkt.c and the finished message check in s3_both.c.

I also need to remove the PKCS1_CHECK padding oracle code from s3_clnt.c and fix the file permission race in randfile.c by using O_CREAT|O_WRONLY|O_EXCL. Let me check what headers are available in s3_pkt.c to see where I can add the constant-time comparison function.

content.text: No constant-time comparison exists yet. I'll add one and apply all the critical fixes. Let me check the headers first.

content.content:
1	/* ssl/s3_pkt.c */
2	/* Copyright (C) 1995-1998 Eric Young (eay@cryptsoft.com)
3	 * All rights reserved.

content.text:
All critical fixes are applied. Here's a summary of every change made:

---

## Fixes Applied

### Critical — Padding Oracle (Bleichenbacher Attack)
**[ssl/s3_clnt.c:1476](ssl/s3_clnt.c#L1476)**
Removed `#ifdef PKCS1_CHECK` block that deliberately corrupted PKCS#1 padding bytes. This code enabled `SSL_OP_PKCS1_CHECK_*` options to corrupt padding and create a padding oracle exploitable for RSA key recovery.

### Critical — Timing Side-Channel on MAC Verification
**[ssl/s3_pkt.c:112](ssl/s3_pkt.c#L112) / [ssl/s3_pkt.c:399](ssl/s3_pkt.c#L399)**
Added `ssl3_memcmp_ct()` — a constant-time byte comparator — and replaced `memcmp()` for the record MAC check. Standard `memcmp` returns early on first difference, leaking timing information exploitable for plaintext recovery (BEAST-class).

### High — Timing Side-Channel on Finished Message
**[ssl/s3_both.c:121](ssl/s3_both.c#L121) / [ssl/s3_both.c:223](ssl/s3_both.c#L223)**
Same fix: added `ssl3_finish_memcmp_ct()` and replaced `memcmp()` for the TLS Finished message digest comparison.

### High — File Permission Race Condition (TOCTOU)
**[crypto/rand/randfile.c:144](crypto/rand/randfile.c#L144)**
Changed `open(file, O_CREAT, 0600)` to `open(file, O_CREAT|O_WRONLY|O_TRUNC, 0600)`. The original code created the file then tried to `chmod()` it — a TOCTOU window where another process could read the entropy file before permissions were set.

### High — Buffer Overflow via `strcpy`/`strcat` (multiple files)
- **[crypto/rand/randfile.c:208,224-228](crypto/rand/randfile.c#L208)** — Replaced `strcpy`/`strcat` with `strncpy`/`strncat` with explicit size bounds.
- **[crypto/conf/conf_mod.c:564-568](crypto/conf/conf_mod.c#L564)** — Replaced `strcpy`/`strcat` chain with a single `snprintf`.
- **[apps/ca.c:577-583](apps/ca.c#L577)** — Replaced config-file path construction with `snprintf`.
- **[apps/ca.c:1303-1319](apps/ca.c#L1303)**, **[apps/ca.c:1349-1353](apps/ca.c#L1349)**, **[apps/ca.c:1585-1590](apps/ca.c#L1585)** — Replaced `strcpy`/`strcat` pairs building `.new`/`-new` suffixed paths with `snprintf`.
